class_schedule.topic: From Posting to Strategy: The Social Media Manager's Role
learning_objectives: Distinguish between tactical social media posting and strategic social media management
Identify the core responsibilities and skills of a social media manager
Map social media objectives to organizational business goals
readings: Sprout Social, 'The State of Social Media' (current year report)

lecture_notes.introduction: Social media management is not about posting content and checking likes. It is a strategic function that connects organizational goals to audience relationships through planned, measured, and optimized communication across platforms.
key_concepts: Strategic vs. tactical social media: strategy defines why and for whom; tactics define what and when
The social media manager role: strategist, content creator, community manager, analyst, and brand guardian
Goal alignment: tying social media objectives (awareness, engagement, conversion, loyalty) to business outcomes
Platform selection strategy: choosing platforms based on audience presence, not trend chasing
Social media maturity model: from reactive posting to integrated strategic communication
lecture_notes.demonstration: Audit of a brand's social media presence across three platforms, mapping observed activity to apparent strategy and identifying gaps between stated goals and actual content
setup_requirements: Access to major social media platforms for observation

review.value: A framework describing organizational social media sophistication from level 1 (ad hoc posting by whoever is available) to level 5 (integrated strategy with dedicated team, measurement, and cross-functional alignment)

The practice of connecting every social media objective (grow followers, increase engagement) to a measurable business outcome (drive website traffic, generate leads, increase brand awareness in target demographic)

class_schedule.topic: Platform Deep Dive: Algorithms and Audience Behavior
learning_objectives: Explain how algorithmic feeds determine content visibility on major platforms
Identify the content types and posting behaviors that each major algorithm rewards
Adapt content strategy to work with rather than against platform algorithms
readings: Later, 'How the Instagram Algorithm Works' (updated guide)

lecture_notes.introduction: Understanding how platform algorithms work is not about gaming the system. It is about understanding the rules of the environment you are communicating in, so your content reaches the audience it was created for.
key_concepts: Algorithmic feed basics: relevance scoring, engagement signals, recency, and relationship strength
Instagram algorithm: initial distribution to a subset, engagement velocity determines wider reach
LinkedIn algorithm: dwell time, comments over likes, professional relevance scoring
TikTok For You Page: interest graph over social graph, completion rate as primary signal
Algorithm-resistant strategies: building direct relationships (email, DMs) to reduce platform dependency
lecture_notes.demonstration: Comparing the reach and engagement of different content types (carousel, video, text post, link post) on the same brand's LinkedIn page over 30 days using publicly available data

review.value: The speed at which a post receives engagement after publication; high early engagement signals algorithmic relevance, triggering wider distribution to non-followers
TikTok's interest graph shows content based on viewing behavior regardless of who you follow, unlike Facebook/Instagram's social graph which prioritizes content from connections

learning_objectives: Build a monthly content calendar balancing evergreen, timely, and campaign content
Plan a multi-platform social media campaign with phased rollout
Allocate content across the marketing funnel: awareness, consideration, conversion, retention
readings: HubSpot, 'Social Media Content Calendar Template' (downloadable guide)

lecture_notes.introduction: Consistency is the foundation of social media success. A content calendar transforms social media from a daily improvisation into a planned communication system where every post serves a strategic purpose.
key_concepts: Content calendar structure: date, platform, content type, copy, visual asset, CTA, funnel stage, status
Content mix framework: the 80/20 rule (80% value, 20% promotion) or 4-1-1 rule
Campaign architecture: teaser phase, launch phase, sustain phase, close phase
Seasonal and cultural calendar integration: holidays, awareness months, industry events
Buffer and flexibility: leaving room for reactive and real-time content
lecture_notes.demonstration: Building a one-month content calendar for a local restaurant across Instagram, Facebook, and TikTok, incorporating a menu launch campaign, weekly content themes, and community engagement posts
setup_requirements: Spreadsheet or project management tool (Notion, Trello, or Asana)

review.value: For every six posts, share four pieces of relevant third-party content, one soft promotional piece (culture, behind-the-scenes), and one hard promotional piece (direct CTA), maintaining audience value while achieving business goals
Structured rollout: teaser (build anticipation), launch (announce with energy), sustain (maintain momentum with varied content), close (recap results and transition to next initiative)

learning_objectives: Develop response protocols for different types of social media interactions
Apply de-escalation techniques to negative comments and complaints
Build a community management playbook for an organization

lecture_notes.introduction: Social media is not a broadcast channel. It is a conversation. Community management is the discipline of nurturing that conversation, turning followers into community members and customers into advocates.
key_concepts: Response time expectations: platform-specific norms and the impact of response speed on brand perception
Triage framework: categorizing incoming interactions as positive, neutral, negative, or crisis
De-escalation protocol: acknowledge, empathize, take offline, resolve, follow up
Proactive engagement: commenting on relevant conversations, not just responding to inbound
User-generated content: encouraging, curating, and crediting community content
lecture_notes.demonstration: Live simulation exercise: students rotate through a mock social media inbox with pre-written scenarios (complaint, praise, question, troll, influencer mention) and practice appropriate responses under time pressure
setup_requirements: Mock social media inbox (provided via LMS simulation tool)

review.value: A structured response framework for negative interactions: acknowledge the issue publicly, express empathy, move the conversation to a private channel, resolve the problem, and follow up to confirm satisfaction
Consumers expect social media responses within 1-2 hours; brands that respond within 15 minutes see significantly higher satisfaction scores and positive sentiment

learning_objectives: Interpret platform-native analytics dashboards to extract actionable insights
Build a monthly social media report that connects metrics to business objectives
Identify content performance patterns and translate them into strategy adjustments
readings: Buffer, 'Social Media Metrics That Matter' (online guide)

lecture_notes.introduction: Data without interpretation is noise. A social media report that lists numbers without explaining what they mean and what to do next is a waste of everyone's time. Analytical thinking is what separates strategic social media management from posting and hoping.
key_concepts: Metric categories: awareness (reach, impressions), engagement (rate, shares, saves), conversion (clicks, sign-ups), advocacy (mentions, UGC)
Platform-native analytics: Instagram Insights, LinkedIn Analytics, TikTok Analytics, Twitter/X Analytics
Report structure: executive summary, KPI performance vs. targets, top/bottom content, insights, recommendations
Benchmarking: comparing performance against industry averages, competitors, and own historical data
Attribution challenges: connecting social media activity to downstream business outcomes
lecture_notes.demonstration: Building a complete monthly social media report from sample analytics data, walking through each section from executive summary to recommendations, discussing how to present findings to stakeholders
setup_requirements: Report template in Google Slides or PowerPoint

review.value: Total engagements (likes + comments + shares + saves) divided by total reach or impressions, expressed as a percentage; the denominator choice affects the number significantly and should be consistent

A recurring trend in which types of content (format, topic, timing, tone) consistently outperform or underperform, identified through systematic analysis of multiple posts over time rather than individual post evaluation

learning_objectives: Explain the role of paid social in a comprehensive social media strategy
Set up audience targeting parameters for a social media ad campaign
Calculate and interpret basic paid social metrics: CPM, CPC, CPA, ROAS
readings: Meta, 'Meta Ads Manager Getting Started Guide'

lecture_notes.introduction: Organic reach on most platforms has declined to the point where paid social is not optional for brands seeking measurable results. Understanding paid social fundamentals is as essential as organic strategy for any social media manager.
key_concepts: Organic vs. paid social: complementary roles in a unified strategy
Ad objectives: awareness, traffic, engagement, leads, conversions, and when to use each
Audience targeting: demographics, interests, behaviors, lookalike audiences, retargeting
Budget allocation: campaign-level vs. ad-set-level budgets, daily vs. lifetime budgets
Key paid metrics: CPM (cost per thousand impressions), CPC (cost per click), CPA (cost per acquisition), ROAS (return on ad spend)
lecture_notes.demonstration: Walking through the complete setup of a Meta Ads campaign in Ads Manager demo mode: selecting an objective, building an audience, setting budget, creating the ad, and interpreting the projected results
setup_requirements: Meta Ads Manager demo account or sandbox access

review.value: Return on Ad Spend: revenue generated from ads divided by ad spend; a ROAS of 4:1 means every $1 spent generated $4 in revenue, the primary measure of paid social profitability

A targeting option that finds new users who resemble an existing audience (customers, email subscribers, website visitors), leveraging platform data to identify similar behavioral and demographic patterns

questions.question: Explain the difference between an interest graph and a social graph in the context of social media algorithms. Which platform primarily uses each, and how does this affect content strategy?
questions.answer: A social graph prioritizes content from people you are connected to (friends, followers), used primarily by Facebook and Instagram. An interest graph prioritizes content based on your viewing behavior and interests regardless of connections, used primarily by TikTok. This affects content strategy because social-graph platforms reward building a loyal follower base that consistently engages, while interest-graph platforms reward creating inherently compelling content that can reach anyone, making it possible for new accounts to achieve massive reach with a single post.

questions.question: What is the 4-1-1 content mix rule, and why is it preferable to posting only promotional content?
questions.answer: The 4-1-1 rule recommends that for every six posts, four should share relevant third-party or value-driven content, one should be a soft promotion (behind-the-scenes, culture), and one should be a direct promotion (product, CTA). It is preferable to all-promotional posting because audiences follow brands for value, not advertisements. A feed that is entirely promotional drives unfollows and reduces engagement rates, which algorithms interpret as low-quality content, further reducing organic reach. The 4-1-1 balance maintains audience trust and engagement while still advancing business goals.

rubric.key: Connects to algorithmic and audience consequences

questions.question: Describe the five steps of a social media de-escalation protocol for handling a public customer complaint.
questions.answer: The five steps are: (1) Acknowledge the issue publicly and promptly, showing you have seen the complaint. (2) Express empathy for the customer's frustration without admitting fault or making excuses. (3) Take the conversation offline by inviting the customer to DM, email, or phone to discuss details privately. (4) Resolve the problem through the private channel with a concrete solution. (5) Follow up after resolution to confirm satisfaction and, when appropriate, invite the customer to update their public comment.

questions.question: A monthly social media report shows that engagement rate dropped from 3.2% to 1.8% while follower count grew by 15%. What might explain this, and what would you recommend?
questions.answer: The engagement rate drop alongside follower growth likely indicates that new followers are less engaged than the existing audience. Possible causes: the follower growth came from a viral post or paid campaign that attracted a broad audience outside the target demographic, or the content mix shifted toward growth-focused content (viral formats) at the expense of community-engaging content. Recommendation: analyze the source of new followers, segment engagement data by follower cohort (new vs. existing), evaluate whether recent content is serving the core audience or chasing reach, and potentially adjust content strategy to re-engage the community while maintaining growth momentum.

rubric.key: Identifies the paradox of growth with declining engagement
Recommends actionable analytical and strategic steps

major_assignments.title: Social Media Strategy and Campaign Execution
major_assignments.description: Develop a comprehensive social media strategy for a real local business or campus organization, then execute a one-week campaign including content creation, community management, and performance reporting. Students will manage the organization's actual social media accounts (with supervision) or create a complete simulated campaign with realistic assets.
objectives: Conduct a social media audit and competitive analysis
Develop a strategic plan with goals, audience personas, platform selection, and content pillars
Execute a one-week campaign with daily content across selected platforms
Analyze campaign performance and deliver an insights-driven report
deliverables: Social media strategy document (10-12 pages) with audit, goals, personas, and content plan
One-week content calendar with all copy, visuals, and scheduling details
Published content or publication-ready content package (minimum 10 posts)
Performance report with metrics analysis, insights, and recommendations

criteria: Audit is thorough and identifies genuine strengths and weaknesses
Goals are SMART and aligned to the organization's business objectives
Audience personas are research-informed and platform selection is justified
Content pillars and posting cadence are realistic and strategically sound
Content is platform-native, visually professional, and copy is error-free
Content mix follows the strategic plan and content calendar
Community management is responsive and follows established protocols
Report includes all relevant metrics with accurate calculations
Data is interpreted with genuine insight rather than merely listed
Recommendations are specific, actionable, and grounded in performance data
Report is professionally formatted and suitable for a client presentation
Comprehensive strategy demonstrating rigorous research and strategic thinking
Professional campaign execution with platform-native content
Data-driven performance report with actionable recommendations
Cohesive project suitable for a professional portfolio

quizzes.description: Regular quizzes on social media strategy concepts and platform knowledge

weekly_platform_exercises.description: Weekly hands-on exercises in content creation, community management, and analytics

major_assignment.description: Comprehensive social media strategy and campaign execution project

midterm_examination.description: Written and practical examination covering strategy development and platform knowledge

final_examination.description: Cumulative examination including a live strategy pitch and analytics interpretation

course_policies.attendance: Attendance is mandatory for all sessions. Social media is a fast-moving field; missing class means missing current examples and discussions that cannot be recreated. More than 3 absences result in a grade reduction.
course_policies.late_submissions: Late assignments lose 10% per day. Campaign content that misses its scheduled publication window cannot be made up and receives zero credit for timeliness.
course_policies.collaboration: Weekly exercises may involve partner work. The major assignment is individual. Sharing strategy documents or content between students constitutes academic dishonesty.
course_policies.academic_integrity: All content must be original or properly licensed. Screenshots and data used in reports must be accurately represented. Fabricating analytics data is academic fraud and grounds for course failure.
course_policies.technology_requirements: Students need accounts on Instagram, LinkedIn, Twitter/X, and TikTok for observation and practice. A laptop with internet access, a smartphone, and access to Canva or equivalent design tool are required.
